data.title: CBOT corn stabilizes a day after hitting 16-month high
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade corn futures finished nearly unchanged on Thursday as the market took a breather after climbing to a 16-month high during the previous session, traders said.

  • Traders are looking for direction from developments in U.S. trade policy, farmers' planting intentions or global weather forecasts.

  • Expectations for an increase in U.S. corn plantings this spring continued to hang over the market, as the crop looks more profitable to produce than soybeans, analysts said.

  • Agricultural lender CoBank projected that U.S. farmers will plant 94.55 million acres to corn in 2025, up about 4% from 2024.

  • The International Grains Council cut its forecast for 2024/25 global production, largely reflecting a diminished outlook for crops in Brazil and Argentina.

  • CBOT March corn (CH25) rose 1/2 cent to end at $4.98 per bushel.

data.title: Security Federal Announces Cash Dividend Increase
data.description:
AIKEN, S.C., Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Security Federal Corporation, parent company of Security Federal Bank, is pleased to announce that a quarterly dividend of $0.15 per share will be paid on or about March 15, 2025, to shareholders of record as of February 28. 2025. This increased dividend represents an increase of $0.01, or 7.1%, over the previous regular quarterly dividend.   

This is the one hundred thirty-seventh consecutive quarterly dividend to shareholders since the Bank’s conversion in October of 1987 from a mutual to a stock form of ownership. The dividend was declared as a result of the Bank’s continued profitability.

Security Federal Bank has nineteen full-service branch locations in Aiken, Ballentine, Clearwater, Columbia, Graniteville, Langley, Lexington, North Augusta, Ridge Spring, Wagener and West Columbia, South Carolina and Augusta and Evans, Georgia. A full range of financial services, including trust and investments, are provided by the Bank, and insurance services are provided by the Bank’s wholly owned subsidiary, Security Federal Insurance, Inc.

Security Federal Corporation common stock is traded on the Over-the-Counter Bulletin Board under the symbol SFDL.

FOR ADDITIONAL INFORMATION CALL:

Beverly S. Nettles – (803) 641-3000

data.title: The stock market looks good from the outside - but inside it's another story
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

The percentage of unprofitable publicly traded U.S. companies is rising

One in eight large-cap companies and 32% of small-caps - about one in three - are unprofitable.

The U.S. stock market looks good from the outside, but inside it's decaying.

It's easy to overlook this internal deterioration as the major market averages are reaching new all-time highs. But the numbers don't lie: An increasing number of publicly traded companies are unprofitable. This trend can't continue indefinitely.

Consider the chart above, courtesy of data from Finomial, the fintech company. The chart shows, for both large-cap and small-cap stocks, the percentage that is unprofitable at any given time. Notice that for each group, the trend over the past two decades is for an increasing share to be unprofitable. Currently, 12% of large-cap companies - roughly one in eight - and 32% of small-caps - about one in three - are unprofitable, despite the U.S. economy being in its 16th year of expansion (overlooking the two-month recession that accompanied the COVID-19 economic lockdown in early 2020).

The unprofitable firms are concentrated among the small caps. The largest companies, in contrast, which have increasingly dominated the market, are more consistently profitable. Many bulls insist that the trends in this chart are not all that alarming.

I'm not so sure. Even though the megacap stocks that dominate the market may be more consistently profitable than the small caps, they still can stumble badly. A market that's dependent on just a few megacaps is vulnerable if several of them stumble at the same time.

If you had any doubt about the market's vulnerability in this regard, consider the largest drawdowns experienced since 2021 by any of the stocks that are part of the "Magnificent Seven" (or their predecessor group, "FAANG"). The market has been fortunate that these stocks didn't simultaneously experience their drawdowns, but there is no guarantee that the market will be so lucky in the coming years.

Skewed p/e ratios

A less-appreciated consequence of the trend toward more widespread unprofitability is how it biases the price/earnings ratio to make the stock market appear to be more reasonably priced than it really is. When a company is losing money, its P/E ratio is negative. When calculating the marketwide P/E ratio, therefore, many data providers simply ignore unprofitable companies - even though there is a process, though more complicated, to incorporate these losses into the calculation.

To appreciate how much the P/E ratio can be biased by ignoring unprofitable companies, consider the Russell 2000 Index RUT, perhaps the most widely-cited benchmark for the small- and midcap sectors of the market. When focusing only on profitable companies, the index's trailing 12-month P/E ratio is 16.57, which on the surface seems reasonable. When properly incorporating unprofitable companies' losses in the calculation, the Russell 2000's P/E more than doubles to 33.89 - in or approaching overvaluation territory.

A healthy stock market is one in which not only are a large percentage of companies profitable, but this percentage is growing. Neither seems to be the case currently.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: U.S. stocks are back at record highs. Is it too late to chase the bull market?

Plus: The biggest U.S. stocks haven't been this expensive since the dot-com era. That's making investors nervous.

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: HUMANA INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Humana Inc., a leading health and well-being company, has released its 2024 10-K report, detailing its financial and operational performance over the past year. The report highlights significant growth in revenue, strategic initiatives aimed at focusing on Medicare Advantage, and the challenges faced in a competitive and regulated industry.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $117,761 million, reflecting a 10.7% increase from the previous year primarily due to higher per member Medicare premiums and membership growth in Medicare Advantage and state-based contracts.

Income from Operations: $2,562 million, a decrease of 36.2% from the previous year, primarily due to higher benefit expenses and lower favorable prior period medical claims reserve development.

Net Income: $1,214 million, a decrease of 51.1% from the previous year, significantly impacted by put/call valuation adjustments, charges associated with value creation initiatives, and impairment charges.

Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: $9.98, a decrease of 50.1% from the previous year, reflecting the same factors impacting net income.

Benefit Ratio: 89.8%, an increase of 2.5 percentage points from the previous year, primarily due to elevated Medicare Advantage and state-based contracts medical cost trends.

Operating Cost Ratio: 11.8%, a decrease of 0.7 percentage points from the previous year, driven by scale efficiencies and administrative cost efficiencies resulting from value creation initiatives.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The Insurance segment, which includes Medicare and state-based contracts, generated significant revenue growth, primarily driven by higher per member Medicare premiums and membership growth in Medicare Advantage and state-based contracts.

Geographical Performance: Florida remains a key market, with approximately 924,800 individual Medicare Advantage members, contributing to 19% of the individual Medicare Advantage premiums revenue.

Sales Units: The company reported a total insurance medical membership of approximately 16.3 million as of December 31, 2024, with notable growth in individual Medicare Advantage membership, which increased by 252,900 members or 4.7% from the previous year.

New Product Launches: The company continues to expand its Medicare Advantage offerings, including Dual-Eligible Special Needs Plans (D-SNP), which saw a net increase of 65,800 members or 7.6% in 2024.

New Production Launches: The CenterWell segment expanded its primary care operations, with 344 primary care clinics and approximately 1,000 primary care providers, serving around 390,500 patients.

Future Outlook: For 2025, the company anticipates a net membership decline in individual Medicare Advantage offerings of approximately 550,000 members, while expecting growth in state-based contracts by 175,000 to 250,000 members.

Operational Efficiency: The company achieved administrative cost efficiencies through value creation initiatives, contributing to a decrease in the operating cost ratio from 12.5% in 2023 to 11.8% in 2024.

Strategic Partnerships: The CenterWell segment's strategic partnership with Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe (WCAS) continues to support the expansion of senior-focused primary care centers, with 133 clinics operating under the partnership as of December 31, 2024.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: Humana Inc. announced its planned exit from the Employer Group Commercial Medical Products business to focus on its Medicare Advantage business and healthcare services expansion. This strategic decision is part of a broader initiative to drive value through cost savings, productivity improvements, and value acceleration from previous investments. The company recorded charges related to asset impairments and severance as part of these initiatives.

Capital Management: Humana engaged in several capital management activities, including issuing $1.3 billion of 5.375% unsecured senior notes due 2031 and $1.0 billion of 5.750% unsecured senior notes due 2054, with net proceeds used for general corporate purposes and debt repayment. The company also repurchased $803 million of its own shares and paid $431 million in dividends to stockholders. Additionally, Humana entered into a securities lending program and an uncommitted receivables purchase facility, providing net proceeds of $418 million and $123 million, respectively.

Future Outlook: Humana plans to finalize its exit from the Employer Group Commercial Medical Products business by the first half of 2025. The company anticipates continued focus on Medicare Advantage and state-based contracts, with expected membership growth in these areas. Humana also intends to maintain significant levels of aggregate excess statutory capital and surplus in its state-regulated operating subsidiaries, ensuring adequate resources for ongoing operations, regulatory requirements, and future expansion opportunities.

Challenges and Risks

Challenges and Risks: Humana Inc. operates in a highly competitive industry with significant reliance on government contracts, particularly Medicare and Medicaid, which account for a substantial portion of its revenue. The company faces challenges in maintaining and expanding its market share due to intense competition and the potential for new entrants, especially in the Medicare program. Additionally, the planned exit from the Employer Group Commercial Medical Products business by 2025 indicates a strategic shift that may impact revenue streams.

Pricing and Cost Management: The company must accurately design and price its products to cover healthcare costs. Failure to do so could materially affect profitability. Factors such as medical cost inflation, increased use of medical services, and changes in government-mandated benefits pose risks to cost management.

Regulatory Risks: Changes in government policies, particularly those affecting Medicare Advantage and Prescription Drug Plans, could impact profitability. Legislative actions that reduce premium payments or increase costs could have adverse effects.

Operational Risks: The company's ability to implement clinical initiatives and manage operating expenses is crucial. Failure to effectively manage these could adversely affect financial results.

Cybersecurity and Data Integrity: Humana's reliance on information systems and third-party service providers exposes it to cybersecurity risks. Breaches could lead to significant regulatory fines, reputational damage, and operational disruptions.

Legal and Compliance Risks: The company is involved in various legal actions and investigations, which could result in substantial damages or changes in business practices if resolved unfavorably.

Market Risks: The decline in Medicare Advantage Star Ratings for 2025 poses a risk to future quality bonus payments and could affect revenues and cash flows.

Management's Focus: Management is focused on executing strategic initiatives, particularly in the Medicare segment, to drive growth. The company is investing in its integrated care delivery model and expanding its CenterWell segment to enhance healthcare services. However, the concentration of revenues in Medicare products intensifies risks associated with regulatory changes and market competition.

Market Volatility: Humana is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations and changes in the securities and credit markets, which could affect the value of its investment portfolio and investment income. The company monitors these risks and believes it has adequate resources to manage them, but ongoing market volatility could impact financial performance.

SEC Filing: HUMANA INC [ HUM ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2025

data.title: Daily Roundup of Key US Economic Data for Feb. 20
data.description:
Initial jobless claims increased by 5,000 to 219,000 in the employment survey week ended Feb. 15, though the four-moving average fell by 1,000 to 215,250, a second straight decrease.

Initial claims were at a level of 223,000 in the employment survey week ended Jan. 18.

Insured claims rose by 24,000 to 1.869 million in the week ended Feb. 8.

The Philadelphia Fed's manufacturing reading fell to 18.1 in February from 44.3 in January, indicating slower, but still solid, expansion. The Empire State manufacturing index released earlier in the week also showed modest growth in February.

The Conference Board's Leading Economic Index fell by 0.3% in January after a 0.1% increase in December, with concerns about future business conditions and a shorter factory workweek were the key factors.

Natural gas stocks fell by 196 billion cubic feet to 2.101 trillion cubic feet in the week ended Feb. 14, down 15.5% from a year earlier and 5.3% lower than the seasonal average for the current week over the previous five years.

Total crude oil inventories rose by 4.6 million barrels in the week ended Feb. 14, with commercial oil inventories up 4.6 million barrels and stocks in the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve unchanged. Gasoline inventories and distillate inventories both decreased.

data.title: Anaergia Up, But Off Day Highs, After Signing LOI with JGC for RNG Project in Japan
data.description:
Anaergia was at last look up $0.04 at $1.07 in mid-afternoon Thursday trade, having touched a session high of $1.09 a short time ago, after its unit, Anaergia Singapore, entered into a letter of intent (LOI) to supply technology and equipment to JGC Holdings for a renewable natural gas project in Japan.

The company said it will provide equipment to JGC under the LOI, including Anaergia's feedstock pretreatment solution, anaerobic digestion with unique digester design, and digestate management solution. Thursday's statement noted the facility is expected to take in more than 56,000 tons per year of cow manure and more than 5,000 tons per year of food waste. These organic waste streams are to be converted into approximately 1,700,000 cubic metres per year of biomethane equivalent of renewable natural gas as well as fertilizer.

"Anaergia's integrated technical solutions will enable JGC to develop a unique facility that will take in two different waste streams and will generate renewal gas that can significantly decarbonize fuel supply," said JGC Executive Vice President Masahiro Aika.

ANRG Shares were last seen up $0.04 at $1.07 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Walmart is the Dow's worst performer today. Analysts aren't nervous.
data.description:
By Bill Peters

'We are not overly concerned with the guidance, and to us, the bottom line is that [Walmart's] business trends remain strong,' D.A. Davidson says

Walmart Inc. was the Dow Jones Industrial Average's worst performer on Thursday, after the big-box retail chain forecast its first year-over-year drop in quarterly profit in three years.

Wall Street analysts, however, weren't sweating it.

In a research note Thursday, D.A. Davidson analyst Michael Baker said that Walmart "has a recent track record of beating, then guiding the next quarter low, then beating that," before "giving initial full-year guidance that ends up being beatable."

"Thus, we are not overly concerned with the guidance, and to us, the bottom line is that WMT's business trends remain strong," he added.

Walmart shares (WMT) were down 6.5% on Thursday. However, that drop followed an extended run higher for the stock, which is still up around 67% over the past 12 months. Heading into Thursday's results, expectations on Wall Street were high.

That broader advance for Walmart's stock has come as consumers of all income levels turn to the chain for a break from three years of price increases following the pandemic's disruptions to the economy, multiple supply shocks and corporate profit-guarding. The chain has expanded its e-commerce and digital-advertising businesses, and grown in categories like health and wellness.

Still, Walmart's results follow a dip in sales at U.S. retailers last month in the wake of the holiday-shopping season. Harsh winter weather in some parts of the country has also kept shoppers from going to stores.

During Walmart's earnings call, executives said the company's core business was strong. Their outlook assumed a "relatively stable macroeconomic environment," while acknowledging "uncertainties" in the economy and consumer behavior.

"We remain confident that Walmart is well positioned to navigate as it has over the last several years, while continuing to deliver value for customers and shareholders alike," Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey said.

David Silverman, senior director at Fitch Ratings, said in emailed commentary that while Walmart still forecast a 3% to 4% sales gain for the fiscal year ahead, trends for retailers could be bumpy this year as lower-income consumers still struggle and tariffs threaten to push prices higher.

"However, Walmart's good operating base and execution should enable it to successfully manage through unpredictability and likely fortify its longer-term market position," he said.

BofA analysts, in a note on Thursday, kept their buy rating on Walmart's stock. They said the chain had potential to grab a bigger portion of the retail market, while boosting profits in its high-margin digital-ad segment - in which brands pay to advertise within Walmart's digital ecosystem - as well as seller fees on its third-party marketplace. They also cited the potential for narrowing losses in its e-commerce business.

Justin McAuliffe, research analyst at Gabelli Funds, suggested in emailed commentary Thursday that Walmart's investors may have gotten ahead of themselves. But he said nothing in the company's results suggested deeper alarm about the consumer.

"I don't think they saw a change in the overall consumer environment causing them to be more cautious," he wrote. "It really sounded like they were following the playbook that they had last year to set expectations and look to improve upon that over the year."

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US Existing Homes Sales Expected to Slow in January
data.description:
The National Association of Realtors' measure of US existing-home sales is expected to slow to a 4.13 million annual rate in January, based on a survey compiled by Bloomberg, after rising by 2.2% to a 4.24 million rate in December.

Existing-home sales were at a 4.00 million rate in January 2024, so the year-over-year change would be positive.

The data are scheduled to be released at 10:00 a.m. ET Friday.

The pending home sales index, also operated by the NAR, fell by 5.5% in December and was down 5% from a year ago. That measure is taken at contract signing and is meant to foreshadow movements in existing homes closings a month or two out.

"A contraction in the index of pending home sales in December raises the possibility of a decline in existing home sales in January following advances in the prior three months," Daiwa said. "In context, constrained inventories coupled with elevated financing costs have contributed to reduced affordability for prospective buyers, thus leaving the current pace of activity in the low end of the longer-term range."

The median sales price was 6% above its year-ago level in December.

Recent mortgage application data from the Mortgage Bankers Association have shown that mortgage rates steadied in late-January.

Home building data for January released on Wednesday showed a decline in housing starts, a small gain in building permits and a rebound in home completions.

data.title: Wall St slides as weak Walmart guidance prompts worries about consumer demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • US-listed shares of Alibaba gain after Q3 revenue beat
  • Baxter International jumps consensus-beating estimates
  • Palantir Technologies drops on potential Pentagon budget cuts
  • Indexes down: Dow 1.28%, S&P 500 0.67%, Nasdaq 0.71%

Wall Street slid on Thursday as ongoing tariff jitters and a downbeat forecast from Walmart dampened investor risk appetite.

A broad selloff pulled all three major U.S. stock indexes sharply lower, with the Dow off more than 1% and the S&P 500 on track to snap its two-day string of record closing highs.

Gold prices surged to a record high, suggesting a flight to safety amid mounting uncertainties.

Walmart , the world's largest retailer, provided current fiscal year sales and profit forecasts that fell shy of analysts' expectations, which suggested dampening consumer demand.

"Walmart tends to cater towards consumers who are most susceptible to inflation and they've done very, very well with huge demand for their products," said Mike Dickson, head of research at Horizon Investments in Charlotte, North Carolina.

"So the weakness in their forecast, combined with last week's pretty bad retail sales data, definitely gives markets a little pause regarding the strength of the consumer."

Walmart's shares slid 6.5%, while other large retailers Target and Costco Wholesale lost 1.8% and 2.2%, respectively.

Walmart's results also provided a glimpse into how the company expects to fare under U.S. President Donald Trump's growing list of tariff announcements.

On Wednesday, that list was expanded to include lumber, autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals.

"You can't talk about uncertainty if you don't mention tariffs, right? That's been a very volatile headline," Dickson added.

"Is Trump using this as a bargaining tool? Is he serious about it? Who's going to be impacted? You've seen a lot of that."

Recent economic data, including jobless claims and Atlantic region factory activity, suggested that the U.S. economy is in solid shape for now, in line with recent comments by Federal Reserve policymakers.

But some economists fear labor market disruptions are possible as a result of the thousands of federal employees recently fired by billionaire Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 572.12 points, or 1.28%, to 44,055.89, the S&P 500 lost 40.99 points, or 0.67%, to 6,103.15 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 141.59 points, or 0.71%, to 19,914.89.

Among the 11 major sectors in the S&P 500, financials were down the most, while energy enjoyed the biggest percentage gain.

Palantir Technologies , which provides governments with services such as software that visualizes army positions, shed 6.9% after the Pentagon announcement on Wednesday that it was looking at potential budget cuts for the fiscal year 2026.

U.S.-listed shares of Alibaba Group advanced 8.2% following the Chinese e-commerce firm's consensus-beating third-quarter revenue.

Hasbro jumped 13.8% after the toymaker beat quarterly profit and revenue estimates.

Medical device maker Baxter International gained 8.5% after the company provided a better-than-expected 2025 profit forecast.

Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.44-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 93 new highs and 67 new lows on the NYSE.

On the Nasdaq, 1,520 stocks rose and 2,807 fell as declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.85-to-1 ratio.

The S&P 500 posted 9 new 52-week highs and 3 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 50 new highs and 119 new lows.

data.title: Bragg Gaming Up 1.5% As Expands Into Quebec With Loto-Quebec Content Deal
data.description:
Bragg Gaming Group's was at last look up 1.5% on Thursday after announcing that it has entered into an online casino content agreement with Canadian operator Loto-Quebec, the only 100% legal and local casino site in Quebec.

The agreement marks the expansion of Bragg into its second province, following Bragg's prior entry into Ontario in March 2022.

Pursuant to the agreement, Loto-Quebec will benefit from access to exclusive content, including titles from Bragg's in-house proprietary content development studios, a roster which includes Atomic Slot Lab and Indigo Magic, through Bragg Gaming's Remote Games Server (RGS) technology, delivered via the Bragg HUB platform.

In addition, Loto-Quebec players will also be able to access on lotoquebec.com titles from multiple content partners under the Powered By Bragg program, including King Show Games, Bluberi, Incredible Technologies and Sega Sammy Creation.

The agreement with Loto-Quebec's online casino supports Bragg Gaming's strategic goals by accelerating the growth of utilization of Bragg's exclusive and proprietary content, and the diversification of revenue through expansion into North American markets.

"Loto-Quebec is a strategic partner, and we are excited to see our proprietary games as well as exclusive content from our partners live and available to Loto-Quebec players," said Bragg's Senior Vice President for Commercial, U.S. and Canada, Garrick Morris. "We look forward to developing our relationship as a key supplier to Loto-Quebec as we continue the roll-out with regular new game releases planned."

data.title: IRS Job Cuts Could Be the Largest at Any Federal Agency — So Far — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

Media reports that the Internal Revenue Service is planning to fire some 6,000 workers this week are raising concerns about tax filing season, which is just ramping up.

The agency, which received an infusion of nearly $60 billion as part of the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act signed by President Joe Biden, used part of that money to boost its ranks of auditors. It announced in October 2023 that it was hiring 3,700 new agents to catch more tax cheats among "high income earners, partnerships, large corporations and promoters."

The agency had 94,562 employees as of last May, including 14,130 who had been there for less than a year, according to federal data. That's 10,000 more than it had in 2014, but the newest data may be inflated since there are more workers during tax season. New workers are most at risk for losing their jobs because they are typically on probation for a year.

The Trump administration has been in the process of firing thousands of workers still in their probationary period across more than a dozen departments and agencies. The recently-terminated employees include 1,000 in Veterans Affairs, 388 in the Environmental Protection Agency, and 170 in the National Weather Service. Other reported dismissals include 2,000 at the Forest Service and thousands more across the Departments of Energy, Homeland Security, Interior, Transportation, and elsewhere.

The latest layoff reports come days after acting IRS commissioner, Michelle King, stepped down after Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency sought access to the agency's data. Such access could include its Integrated Data Retrieval System, which stores personal taxpayer information.

Fears of tax season disruption may be overblown. When the government sought volunteers for its deferred-resignation program earlier this month, IRS workers deemed critical to tax season were told that they need to work at least through mid-May. About 75,000 federal employees accepted the offer across all federal agencies.

That said, the IRS isn't known for speedy response times or promptly answering calls. While it has made efforts to improve its customer service, the cuts could affect it if some of those roles are eliminated.

The IRS began accepting 2024 tax returns on Jan. 27, two days earlier than in 2023. It has processed nearly 24 million returns, about 17% of the more than 140 million it expects to receive by the April 15 deadline.

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Apple's iPhone 16e Is a Big Moment for the Company's Own Chip. What It Means for Qualcomm Stock. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Apple's newest iPhones will be the first to use the company's own modems, a monumental change — and a potentially big positive — for the Cupertino-based tech giant.

Apple debuted the iPhone 16e on Wednesday, a cheaper, slimmed-down version of the iPhone 16. Starting at $599, it's the least expensive smartphone option for people who want access to the company's Apple Intelligence suite of artificial intelligence applications. The phone will be available for preorder on Feb. 21.

One of the biggest differences with the 16e is its modem chip, which helps the phone connect to wireless data networks. The 16e is the first iPhone to use the first cellular modem designed by Apple, called C1. Other iPhones included modems designed by Qualcomm.

Apple said the C1 is the "most power-efficient modem ever on an iPhone," and helps contribute to improved battery life on the 16e.

Wall Street has been expecting Apple to veer away from its partnership with Qualcomm for some time now. The C1 announcement made this announcement "the biggest news to us," Morningstar analyst William Kerwin wrote in a note on Wednesday, adding that he expects in-house modems to be an integral part of new iPhone releases in a few years.

Making its own modems could be a positive for Apple in the long run as it would eventually save money on licensing deals with outside parties. BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote on Wednesday that he believes "the shift to in-house production of modems (and other future components) will drive GM's [gross margins] higher overtime."

TD Cowen analyst Krish Sankar wrote that this could be the start of a multiyear transition away from the partnership with Qualcomm. Sankar said it could benefit iPhone margins "as we estimate modem cost savings of 45%."

In 2023, Qualcomm announced that it reached an agreement with Apple to make it the supplier of 5G chips for iPhones until 2026. This deal has been an important one for Qualcomm, which said in its latest 10-K filing that in fiscal 2024, revenues from Apple, Samsung, and Xiaomi each comprised 10% or more of its consolidated revenues.

But it hasn't been a secret that Apple has wanted to make its own chips. In 2019, the tech giant acquired Intel's modem chip business for $1 billion to fast-track those efforts.

"Our business, particularly our semiconductor business, may suffer as a result of our customers vertically integrating (i.e., developing their own integrated circuit products)," Qualcomm also said in its 10-K in November.

KeyBanc analyst John Vinh wrote in a note on Wednesday that he thinks Apple will try to use its internal modem in about 40 million iPhones in 2025, 100 million in 2026, and 200 million in 2027.

"We estimate the full impact of AAPL internalizing its 5G modem will represent a $1.55-$1.65 EPS headwind to earnings for QCOM," Vinh said.

A Qualcomm spokesperson said to Barron's that "the best commercial smartphones currently feature our leading 5G-Advanced modem-RF system, which uses AI to optimize for the fastest and highest-capacity networks in every country."

Qualcomm wants to show it has a future without Apple. At its investor's day in November, the company said it was targeting revenue of $4 billion from its personal-computer business by 2029.

"The Qualcomm today is very different than the Qualcomm from the past, and hopefully you'll be able to see it, as we expanded and became a partner to industries beyond mobile; in automotive, in personal computing, in spatial computing," Chief Executive Cristiano Amon said at the investor day.

Qualcomm has telegraphed this well, which may be why shares gained after the iPhone announcement on Wednesday. And the new iPhones with these chips aren't in anyone's pockets, yet.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Reform Partners with Bit2Me to Transform Market Making in Spanish and Portuguese Markets
data.description:
Gibraltar, British Overseas Territory , Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) —  Reform, a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO), is partnering with Bit2Me, the safest and easiest crypto platform within the Spanish and Portuguese markets. By partnering with local exchanges, Reform is expanding its reach within the Spanish and Portuguese-speaking markets to provide accessibility, transparency, and equity for everyone regarding cryptocurrency market making.

Market makers are vital for ensuring liquidity and smooth trading across crypto exchanges, representing a multi-billion-dollar industry. Reform DAO’s structure democratizes this traditionally exclusive role, enabling RFRM token holders to benefit from trading rebates, buybacks, and a user-friendly Telegram tool that generates consistent revenue.

In the first six months, the DAO facilitated over $8,000,000,000 in trading volume and 250,000,000 trades, generating more than $500,000 in net revenue. This underscores the tangible rewards of its groundbreaking model.

“We’re incredibly excited to partner with Bit2Me and bring Reform’s unique approach to market-making to the Spanish and Portuguese-speaking communities,” says Jess Muntenaar, Core Contributor at Reform. “These markets have a vibrant crypto ecosystem, and we believe Reform can play a crucial role in fostering its continued growth.”

Paul Lafaille, Core Contributor at Reform adds, “Reform is not just about providing liquidity; it's about empowering communities and projects. We're building a more equitable and sustainable future, not just for DeFi, but for the entire crypto industry.”

Unlike centralized entities that often offer unfavorable terms to projects, Reform utilizes advanced, AI-driven algorithms to ensure fair and equitable market making. This benefits both project buyers and the projects themselves. Furthermore, Reform is committed to transparency, providing advanced dashboards that offer insights into its operations and fostering a more open ecosystem.

The Reform Advantage:

  • DAO-driven: Reform is the first market making and liquidity providing DAO, aligning incentives across all stakeholders.
  • AI-powered: Reform leverages advanced algorithms to ensure efficient market-making for projects.
  • Transparent: Reform provides advanced dashboards for full transparency and insights into its operations.
  • Community-focused: RFRM token holders benefit from the rewards generated by Reform's activities.
  • Expanding Reach: Strategic partnerships like the one with Bit2Me are extending Reform’s reach into key markets.

About Reform DAO

Reform is the first decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) focused on market making and liquidity provision in the cryptocurrency industry. By leveraging AI technology and aligning the interests of all stakeholders, Reform aims to make crypto market making accessible, transparent, and equitable.

Media Contact

Robert Penington

robert@thronepr.com

data.title: Long Liquidation Pressures Hammer Coffee Prices
data.description:
March arabica coffee (KCH25) Thursday closed down -22.15 (-5.30%), and March ICE robusta coffee (RMH25) closed down -115 (-2.00%).

Coffee prices tumbled Thursday, with arabica falling sharply to a 2-week low. A rebound in arabica coffee inventories sparked long liquidation pressures in arabica coffee futures Thursday. ICE-monitored arabica inventories have rebounded +2.7% higher over the past two sessions to 779,063 bags on Thursday, recovering from Tuesday's 9-month low of 758,514 bags.

Meanwhile, ICE-monitored robusta coffee inventories posted a 4-3/4 month high on January 31 of 4,603 lots but then fell to a 1-3/4 month low of 4,297 lots on Tuesday. Robusta inventories were at 4,347 lots Thursday.

In a bullish factor, an increased percentage of Brazil's coffee harvest has already been sold compared with previous years, meaning there is less supply still available to sell. Safras & Mercado reported Monday that producers sold 88% of Brazil's 2024/25 coffee harvest as of February 11, faster than last year's comparable year-earlier figure of 79% and the 5-year average of 82%. Meanwhile, sales of the 2025/26 crop have been slow at 13% of the crop, well behind the 4-year average of 22%, which suggests a lack of new supply and an unwillingness of producers to sell.

Continued supply fears have supported coffee prices. Cecafe last Wednesday reported that Brazil's January green coffee exports fell -1.6% y/y to 3.98 million bags. Also, on January 28, Conab, Brazil's government crop forecasting agency, forecasted that Brazil's 2025/26 coffee crop would fall -4.4% y/y to a 3-year low of 51.81 million bags. Conab also cut its 2024 Brazil coffee crop estimate by -1.1% to 54.2 million bags from a September estimate of 54.8 million bags.

Below-normal rain in Brazil has heightened coffee crop concerns and is supporting coffee prices after Somar Meteorologia reported Monday that Brazil's biggest arabica coffee growing area of Minas Gerais received 12.4 mm of rain last week, or 20% of the historical average. Brazil is the world's biggest arabica coffee growing country.

The impact of dry El Nino weather last year may lead to longer-term coffee crop damage in South and Central America. Rainfall in Brazil has consistently been below average since last April, damaging coffee trees during the all-important flowering stage and reducing the prospects for Brazil's 2025/26 arabica coffee crop. Brazil has been facing the driest weather since 1981, according to the natural disaster monitoring center Cemaden. Also, Colombia, the world's second-largest arabica producer, is slowly recovering from the El Nino-spurred drought last year.

Robusta coffee prices are underpinned by reduced robusta production. Due to drought, Vietnam's coffee production in the 2023/24 crop year dropped by -20% to 1.472 MMT, the smallest crop in four years. The USDA FAS on May 31 projected that Vietnam's robusta coffee production in the new marketing year of 2024/25 will dip slightly to 27.9 million bags from 28 million bags in the 2023/24 season. In addition, Vietnam's General Statistics Office reported on January 10 that 2024 Vietnam coffee exports fell -17.1% y/y to 1.35 MMT. Conversely, the Vietnam Coffee and Cocoa Association on December 3 raised its 2024/25 Vietnam coffee production estimate to 28 million bags from an October estimate of 27 million bags.

News of larger global coffee exports is bearish for prices. Conab reported on February 4 that Brazil's 2024 coffee exports rose +28.8% y/y to a record 50.5 million bags. Also, Vietnam's General Statistics Office reported on February 6 that Vietnam's Jan coffee exports rose +6.3% m/m to 134,000 MT. Vietnam is the world's largest producer of robusta coffee. However, ICO reported on February 6 that Dec global coffee exports fell -12.4% y/y to 10.73 million bags, and Oct-Dec global coffee exports fell -0.8% y/y to 32.25 million bags.

The USDA's biannual report on December 18 was mixed for coffee prices. The USDA's Foreign Agriculture Service (FAS) projected that world coffee production in 2024/25 will increase +4.0% y/y to 174.855 million bags, with a +1.5% increase in arabica production to 97.845 million bags and a +7.5% increase in robusta production to 77.01 million bags. The USDA's FAS forecasts that 2024/25 ending stocks will fall by -6.6% to a 25-year low of 20.867 million bags from 22.347 million bags in 2023/24. Separately, the USDA's FAS on November 22 projected Brazil's 2024/25 coffee production at 66.4 MMT, below its previous forecast of 69.9 MMT. The USDA's FAS projects Brazil's coffee inventories at 1.2 million bags at the end of the 2024/25 season in June, down -26% y/y.

For the 2025/26 marketing year, Volcafe on December 17 cut its 2025/26 Brazil arabica coffee production estimate to 34.4 million bags, down by about 11 million bags from a September estimate after a crop tour revealed the severity of an extended drought in Brazil. Volcafe projects a global 2025/26 arabica coffee deficit of -8.5 million bags, wider than the -5.5 million bag deficit for 2024/25 and the fifth consecutive year of deficits.

On the date of publication, Rich Asplund did not have (either directly or indirectly) positions in any of the securities mentioned in this article. All information and data in this article is solely for informational purposes. For more information please view the Barchart Disclosure Policyhere.

  • Why Tight Corporate-U.S. Treasury Yield Spreads Signal Good News for Ag Markets Prices
  • Did We See Major Changes in Markets from Tuesday Morning to Pre-Dawn Wednesday?

data.title: Walmart Shares Drop After Q4 Earnings, Despite Revenue Growth
data.description:
Walmart (WMT, Financials) shares fell 6.3% to $97.45 as of 2:02 p.m. GMT-5 after the company reported a 5% increase in fourth-quarter revenue to $178.9 billion.

Although the retailer observed success in e-commerce, membership, marketplace, and advertising, investor mood was impacted by questions about margin pressures and economic sensitivity.

Driven by grocery growth, Walmart U.S. comparable sales increased 4.6%; e-commerce sales jumped 16%. Improved inventory control helped to widen 53 basis point gross margins. Revenue from the market grew 37%; Walmart Fulfilment Services completed 45% of orders. Adjusted profits per share came out to be $0.66, somewhat above predictions of $0.65 by analysts.

With adjusted EPS range of $2.50-$2.60, Walmart forecasts 3%-4% consolidated net sales growth and 3.5%-5.5% operating income growth for fiscal 2026. The business anticipates headwinds in currencies to lower operating income by 150 basis points and sales growth by 100 basis points.

Analysts asked about margin durability and economic sensitivity. Excluding the leap year and the VIZIO acquisition, UBS analysts acknowledged management's trust in wide revenue sources and expected 5%-7% adjusted EBIT growth. While Morgan Stanley analysts asked about e-commerce profitability with Walmart's CFO referencing an 11% incremental margin, Goldman Sachs expressed worries about gross margins.

Inflationary pressures, wage investments, and possible tariff effects kept investors wary even with robust e-commerce and advertising growth.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch for Feb. 20: FOMC Need to Remain Vigilant Amid Uncertainty, Rising Inflation Still Possible, Fed Officials Say
data.description:
Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic (nonvoter) said in an essay that the US economy remains strong, but the Federal Open Market Committee needs to remain vigilant in the face of uncertainties that could change that firm footing.

St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem (voter) said that he is concerned about rising inflation expectations as well as changes in fiscal policy that could require the FOMC to act by keeping policy restrictive for longer than expected or even tightening policy. He suggested that the risk of stagflation — a combination of high inflation and slow growth — could not be ruled out.

Recent comments of note:

(Feb. 19) Fed Vice Chair Philip Jefferson (voter) echoed comments from other Fed officials, saying that the FOMC can be patient when considering the timing of the next rate reduction.

(Feb. 19) Minutes of the Jan. 28-29 FOMC meeting showed that participants believe they were 'well positioned' to pause rate reductions given the uncertainty of the fiscal policy outlook and still elevated inflation. The minute said that the FOMC would like to see further progress on inflation before considering further rate reductions, particularly given the uncertainty surrounding fiscal policy decisions. The data released since the meeting have provided much assurance that inflation is slowing, and in fact, have shown an acceleration in consumer price gains.

(Feb. 18) San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly (nonvoter) said that monetary policy should not be eased further until there is additional progress on reducing inflation.

(Feb. 17) Fed Governor Michelle Bowman (voter) said that she would like to see more progress on inflation before considering further rate reduction, citing greater risks to price stability as the labor market remains strong.

(Feb. 17) Fed Governor Christopher Waller (voter) said that tariffs should have only a modest impact on prices, and only a temporary impact in any cases, so those effects should not impede the FOMC from making monetary policy decisions.

(Feb. 17) Philadelphia Fed President Patrick Harker (nonvoter) said that inflation remains elevated, but that it is moving in the correct direction and that, combined with a balanced labor market, suggests that the FOMC should hold rates steady.

data.title: Germany's chancellor frontrunner Merz says US could slide into populist instability
data.description:
Germany's conservative leader Friedrich Merz said on Thursday the U.S. was at risk of sliding into longer-term authoritarian instability, casting doubt on Washington's presence for Germany’s 70th NATO membership anniversary in May.

The outgoing government of Chancellor Olaf Scholz and likely successor Merz have largely avoided direct public criticism of President Donald Trump's month-old administration. But senior U.S. officials' remarks on NATO and free speech in Europe over the past week have been condemned by Berlin.

Merz is candidate to be chancellor for the two allied conservative parties (CDU/CSU) that are leading polls ahead of a snap federal election on Sunday.

"I hope that it (the U.S.) remains a democracy and does not slide into an authoritarian populist system," Merz said at a campaign event in Darmstadt.

He said that if the U.S. remained a democracy, it would need partners as only autocratic systems operated alone.

"But it may be that America will enter a longer period of instability and that this populism, this autocratic behaviour of the heads of state, will continue for a longer period of time," he said, calling for Germany and Europe to step up to become able to defend themselves.

Merz said he was not certain it would be possible to celebrate Germany's 70 years of NATO membership as the political order it had been used to was crumbling.

"Will the Americans still be there? Eight weeks ago I would not have dared to ask this question, but today we have to give an answer to it," he said.

data.title: U.S. Farmers Expected to Plant More Corn — Market Talk
data.description:
1435 ET - U.S. farmers are expected to plant more acres of corn, at the expense of soybean, wheat, cotton and grain sorghum, CoBank said. Tanner Ehmke, grains and oilseeds economist with CoBank, said "While farmers tend to stick to historical crop rotations for agronomic reasons and market diversification, corn's price rally relative to other crops suggests a major shift in acreage is in the offing." A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says corn acres are expected to climb 4.2%, to 94.5 million in 2025. "However, the potential for trade disputes with Canada and Mexico could significantly weaken the demand for U.S. corn and curb the increase in planted acreage," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1431 ET - Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten isn't sure the company will hit its fiscal 2025 outlook. The consumer-products company was banking on the second half of its fiscal year to be stronger than the first, but the macro environment continues to be challenging and has yet to fundamentally improve, Schulten says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference. Tariffs, both enacted and proposed, are introducing new layers of volatility, he adds. The company is taking steps to support the low end of its outlook, but "there is some level of probability that external factors may lead to a slightly below guidance range outcome on the bottom line," Schulten says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1417 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem says that signs from interest-rate-sensitive sectors make it clear that the Fed's policy stance is still restricting the economy after a percentage point of interest-rate cuts in 2024. Slow activity in the housing sector and other industries that rise and fall with financing costs show that monetary policy remains at least a bit tight, Musalem says. "Low- and moderate-income households, and small businesses are still feeling the effects of interest rates and financing conditions," Musalem says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1412 ET - Hasbro's licensing business is a huge margin driver that's relatively unexposed to the "tariff drama" spurred by the White House, CEO Chris Cocks says on a call with analysts. The toymaker's licensing unit has grown by 60% in the last three years, making Hasbro the third largest entertainment licensor globally, and the biggest in the digital games space, Cocks says. Hasbro plans to lean into licensing, and particularly into joint ventures and partnerships for its self-published portfolio in the years to come, the CEO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1344 ET - Hasbro says half of its U.S. toy and game volume originates from China, but the company is working to cut that contribution down to under 40% over the next two years, CFO Gina Goetter says on a call with analysts. The shift comes as the company maneuvers to deal with Trump's new tariffs on Chinese imports and potential levies on imports from Mexico and Canada. Goetter says the company doesn't source from Canada and has minimal sourcing from Mexico. "We're really watching that China rate, that's what we baked in," the CFO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1327 ET - There are compelling reasons for the Bank of Canada to cut again in March, says National Bank Financial economist Taylor Schleich. Traders have scaled back expectations for a 7th straight rate cut next month, with only one in three believing it will happen. Solid hiring, firmer inflation and less fear about hefty tariffs from President Trump are pushing people toward a March pause. Schleich says slack remains in the economy. As for hiring, Schleich says the job vacancy rate is at a 7-year low and the less timely Canada nonfarm payroll data suggests hiring remains tepid. Finally, Schleich says Trump's tariff threats alone are impactful, citing surveys from BOC and other business groups indicating executives are deferring investment, halting hiring plans, and mulling a move to the US. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1326 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem, a voter on the central bank's policy committee this year, says monetary policy is less restrictive than it was before last year's rate cuts, but is still working to restrain the economy modestly. Speaking at an Economic Club of New York meeting in Manhattan, Musalem says that it will be appropriate to keep policy restrictive if inflation stays above the Fed's 2% target. On the other hand, lower rates could be the right move is the labor market worsens and inflation expectations stay anchored, he adds. He presents an optimistic scenario as his baseline: "Looking ahead, I expect inflation will continue to converge to the FOMC's 2% target and the labor market will remain near full employment," he says, referring to the Federal Open Markets Committee that sets monetary policy. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1254 ET - Mexico's reliance on U.S. piped gas will continue to grow, Fitch Ratings says, even as uncertainties over bilateral trade relations increase. U.S. gas imports provide reliable and cost-effective energy for Mexico, buoyed by rising demand, constrained domestic production, and pipeline infrastructure expansion, Fitch says. The market share of U.S. piped gas in Mexico is expected to increase in the coming years, according to Fitch. Trade uncertainties have emerged since President Trump came into office, but the administration has mainly focused on Mexico's exports to the U.S., and Fitch says it sees policy-driven disruptions to the gas trade as a smaller risk. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1219 ET - Walmart set out a 3% to 4% revenue growth target for 2025 that would imply continued share gains given overall retail sales growth is expected to be more modest, Fitch Ratings' David Silverman says in a research note. Those gains would come despite Walmart's already massive scale and be largely driven by strong customer connections and the company's robust omnichannel infrastructure, Silverman says. Fitch is expecting retail choppiness throughout 2025 as consumer sentiment moderates, particularly for lower-income customers, and headwinds like tariffs put pressure on prices, though Walmart should be able to manage the unpredictability, Silverman says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1127 ET - Canada's oil producers are likely to prove resilient in the face of President Trump's threatened 10% tariff on all U.S.-bound Canadian energy exports, S&P Global Ratings says. It notes many U.S. refiners rely on Canadian crude imports as refining feedstock, though most Canadian oil and gas companies have limited alternative sale markets and lack sufficient domestic refining capacity. S&P expects the financial burden of tariffs would be shared by U.S. refiners, and ultimately American consumers, and Canadian producers. And it adds the relatively weak Canadian dollar will limit top-line effects for Canadian producers, while the industry's low debt leverage will further support credit metric resilience and ratings. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1116 ET - U.S. median home-sale prices rose 3.7% year over year in the four weeks ending Feb. 16, the smallest gain since September, Redfin says. Mortgage rates fell to 6.87%, the year's lowest, easing costs slightly. Inventory rose to five months' supply, up from 4.1 last year, the highest since early 2019, as listings increase and sales slow. New listings jumped 4.2%, hitting a three-year peak. Homes sell for 2% below asking price--the biggest discount in two years--and take 57 days to go under contract, the longest in five. Buyers gained negotiating power, especially in buyer-friendly markets like coastal Florida, though West Coast and Northeast sellers still dominate. With home-touring up, spring may shift dynamics. Updated, well-priced homes in top areas will sell quickly, often above asking, while others linger, giving buyers leverage. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1111 ET - Freshpet is sharply lower as 4Q sales growth and its outlook come up short of analyst estimates. But Truist thinks investors may be overreacting. "Recall, the company manages top line growth to avoid supply chain constraints so it rarely blows past the Street's top line estimates," the analysts say. "2024 was an especially impressive year from a margin expansion perspective... and we expect the outlook for more modest expansion in 2025 to prove conservative." Shares of the pet-food producer tumble 13%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Update: WTI Oil Rises Even as a Report Shows U.S. Inventories Rose More Than Expected Last Week
data.description:
West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil closed higher on Thursday despite a report showing another big rise in U.S. inventories last week.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil for March delivery was last seen up US$0.17 to US$72.42 per barrel, while April Brent crude was up US$0.33 to US$76.37.

In its weekly survey released the Energy Information Administration said U.S. commercial crude inventories rose by 4.6-million barrels last wee, more than double the consensus estimate for a rise of 2.2-million barrels among analysts polled by Reuters.

The rise in U.S. oil stocks is a bearish note in a market being supported by pipeline outages and tightening sanctions on exports from Russia and Iran. But despite short-term supply and demand issues, prices have steadily weakened after rising above US$80 per barrel in mid-January, with WTI oil down 4.8% over the past month.

"The market continues to lack a clear direction, with supply disruptions in Kazakhstan and the OPEC+ production increase delay being offset by global demand worries," Saxo Bank noted.

data.title: Netflix eases after announcing $1 bln investment in Mexico
data.description:
** Netflix on Thur announces $1 bln investment to produce ~20 films and TV series annually in Mexico over next 4 years; stock down ~2%

** Dip in NFLX shares follows a record high close on Feb. 14, and stock remains up ~17% since the co reported record subscriber gains on Jan. 21

** NFLX has history of successful Mexican productions, including "Roma", "Club de Cuervos" and "Pedro Paramo," and co plans to collaborate with Mexican movie studio Estudios Churubusco ** NFLX stock has gained about 15% YTD, outperforming Nasdaq 's 3% rise ** NFLX stock recently traded at 41x expected earnings, above its 5-year avg forward PE of 38, per LSEG data

data.title: Enbridge's Michigan Tunnel Pipeline Project Can Continue, Appeals Court Rules
data.description:
Enbridge can continue its plan to build a protective tunnel in the Great Lakes area, the Michigan Court of Appeals ruled after environmental groups and tribes challenged the project's approval.

The court upheld the Michigan Public Service Commission's decision to issue permits for the project, according to a decision published Wednesday.

The groups and tribes had alleged that the commission acted inconsistently in approving the project with regard to evidence regarding the public need for continued operation of the pipeline, according to the ruling.

Enbridge is planning to build a protective tunnel around a section of its Line 5 pipeline beneath the Straits of Mackinac, which link Lake Michigan and Lake Huron.

data.title: Equities Fall Intraday After Latest Macro Data; Walmart Declines on Weak Forecast
data.description:
US equity indexes fell intraday Thursday as markets evaluated the latest macro data and corporate news that included a weak earnings outlook from Walmart (WMT).

The Nasdaq Composite and the S&P 500 each fell about 0.8% to 19,897.2 and 6,096.8, respectively. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was down 1.4% at 43,987. Financials, consumer discretionary, and industrials led decliners among sectors, while energy paced the sector gainers.

In economic news, initial jobless claims rose to 219,000 during the week ended Feb. 15, above the 215,000 level projected in a survey of analysts compiled by Bloomberg. For the week ended Feb. 8, seasonally adjusted continuing claims totaled nearly 1.87 million, up 24,000 from the previous week's downwardly revised level.

The data are "consistent with a labor market that can withstand a (Federal Reserve) on hold as it assesses progress on inflation," Oxford Economics Lead US Economist Nancy Vanden Houten said in emailed remarks. Mass layoffs among federal workers "pose some downside risk to our forecast for the labor market this year," she said.

Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic said area businesses are concerned that tariffs will increase costs as they're prepared to raise prices for consumers to mitigate the impact. They also expressed concern that mass deportations in the US could reduce labor supply, particularly in home construction and leisure and hospitality, he said.

"In recent weeks, we've heard not only enthusiasm — particularly from banks, about possible shifts in tax and regulatory policies — but also widespread apprehension about future trade and immigration policy," Bostic wrote in an essay. "These crosscurrents inject still more complexity into policymaking."

In other economic news, the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia's monthly manufacturing index fell to 18.1 in February from 44.3 in January but outperformed expectations for a 14.3-level in a Bloomberg survey.

The 10-year US Treasury yield fell 3.7 basis points to 4.5%, while the two-year rate was little changed at 4.26%.

In company news, shares of Walmart (WMT) declined 6.5% intraday, the biggest laggard on the Dow, after the retail giant issued a full-year earnings outlook below market estimates, pointing to uncertain consumer behavior and geopolitical conditions. Merck (MRK) led the Dow's gainers, up 1.8%.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) fell 7.8%, among the steepest decliners on the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq, after plunging 10% on Wednesday, amid concerns that the Department of Defense's fiscal 2026 budget cuts would impact the company. Wedbush Securities reiterated an outperform rating on the stock Thursday, saying those fears are overblown as the company is poised to lead the "AI arms race."

EPAM Systems (EPAM) sank 15% intraday, the worst performer on the S&P 500, following a weaker-than-expected adjusted earnings per share outlook.

Gold futures advanced 0.7% to $2,957.5 per troy ounce, while silver jumped 1.3% to $33.49 per troy ounce.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures rose 0.9% to $72.92 a barrel.

data.title: Star Bulk Carriers on Pace for Largest Percent Increase Since February 2023 — Data Talk
data.description:
Star Bulk Carriers Corp. (SBLK) is currently at $15.91, up $0.95 or 6.35%

  • Currently up five of the past six days

All data as of 2:28:10 PM ET
